name: Google Puppeteer
what_it_does: Puppeteer is an open-source Node.js library maintained by Google that controls headless Chrome or Chromium via the Chrome DevTools Protocol (CDP) — not a REST API.
use_cases: Scraping JavaScript-heavy web pages by rendering them in a real Chrome instance
Generating PDFs and screenshots of web pages for document processing pipelines
Automating form submissions, logins, and multi-step web workflows for AI agents
Running Lighthouse performance audits programmatically as part of an agent quality check
Connecting to remote Chrome instances or managed cloud browser services via CDP for scalable scraping
not_for: Cross-browser testing requiring Firefox or WebKit support — Puppeteer is Chrome/Chromium-only
Teams needing a managed REST API or hosted cloud service with no local runtime dependency
Python-first environments where native bindings are preferred — use Playwright for Python
best_when: Best when you need battle-tested Chrome automation in a Node.js environment and want tight integration with Chrome DevTools Protocol features like network interception and performance tracing.
avoid_when: Avoid when you need multi-browser support or a fully managed cloud browser service — use Playwright or Browserbase instead.

auth.notes: No authentication required. Puppeteer is installed as an npm package. Authentication for remote Chrome instances or cloud browser providers is handled at the connection level via WebSocket URLs or API keys passed to puppeteer.connect().

pricing.free_tier_limits: Unlimited — fully open source under the Apache 2.0 license
pricing.paid_tiers: None — the library itself is free. Cloud providers (Browserless, Browserbase) that expose a Puppeteer-compatible CDP endpoint have their own pricing.
pricing.notes: Puppeteer itself has no cost. The npm package bundles a compatible Chromium build by default (~300MB download). Use puppeteer-core to skip the bundled browser and connect to an existing Chrome instance.

performance.rate_limits: No rate limits — constrained only by local Chrome instance capacity or the cloud provider being used

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Error messages include selector context and timeout values but async stack traces are often truncated, making it hard for agents to identify root cause in deeply nested automation chains.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Browser state is mutable and actions like clicks and form submissions are not idempotent. Agents must explicitly manage page state and use fresh browser contexts for repeatable runs.
known_agent_gotchas: Puppeteer downloads a pinned Chromium version (~300MB) at install time; in agent containers this can cause slow cold starts or disk quota issues — use puppeteer-core to avoid this
Page.evaluate() executes code in the browser's JS context, not Node.js — variables and functions from the agent scope are not accessible without explicit argument passing
Network requests intercepted via page.setRequestInterception() must be explicitly continued or aborted; forgetting to call request.continue() hangs the page indefinitely
Chrome DevTools Protocol connections drop silently on browser crash — agents must implement reconnection logic or use a supervisor process
Headless mode detection by anti-bot systems is well-documented; agents doing scraping without stealth plugins will be blocked on many major sites

security_score_components.security_notes: Puppeteer launches Chrome with the full privileges of the Node.js process. Running without a sandbox (--no-sandbox flag commonly required in Docker) removes OS-level isolation. Agents should sandbox the entire Puppeteer process rather than relying on Chrome's internal sandbox.
